options.markscheme: Incorrect. In autosomal recessive inheritance, both males and females must inherit two copies of the allele to express the phenotype.
Incorrect. Codominance involves the full and simultaneous expression of both alleles in the phenotype of heterozygous individuals.
Incorrect. Incomplete dominance results in a heterozygous phenotype that is an intermediate blend of the two homozygous phenotypes.

Correct. In X-linked recessive inheritance, males (who have only one X chromosome) express the trait if they inherit a single recessive allele. Females can be carriers (heterozygous) without showing the phenotype because the dominant allele on their second X chromosome masks the recessive one.

parts.content: Explain why diploid cells have two copies of each autosomal gene.
parts.markscheme:
- Diploid cells receive one set of chromosomes from each parent (via fertilisation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These sets pair up to form homologous chromosome pairs in the nucleus. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each homologous pair therefore carries two copies (alleles) of each autosomal gene at the same loci, one on each homolog. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Meiosis produces haploid gametes, so fusion of gametes restores the diploid number and two gene copies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Autosomes (non-sex chromosomes) are present as homologous pairs in diploid cells. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two differences between haemophilia A and haemophilia B shown in the figure.
parts.markscheme:
- Haemophilia A is more common (1 in 5,000 males) than haemophilia B (1 in 30,000 males). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Haemophilia A involves mutations in the $F8$ gene affecting clotting factor VIII, whereas haemophilia B involves mutations in the $F9$ gene affecting clotting factor IX. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Haemophilia A (factor VIII) is described as a co-factor, whereas haemophilia B (factor IX) is described as an enzyme. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The molecular weight of factor VIII (280 kDa) is greater than factor IX (55 kDa). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The normal plasma concentration range shown differs for factor VIII (0.1–0.25 µg/mL) and factor IX (3–5 µg/mL). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain, with reference to the mutation types shown in the figure, how mutation type could influence the severity or treatment of haemophilia.
parts.markscheme:
- Different mutation types (e.g., nonsense, missense, deletion/insertion, splice-site, inversion) can affect the amount and/or function of the clotting factor produced (complete loss vs partial function). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Severe disease is more likely when little or no functional factor is produced (e.g., disruptive mutations such as nonsense, large deletions, inversions). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some mutations may still allow production of a partly functional protein (e.g., some missense mutations), potentially leading to milder symptoms. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mutation type can affect treatment response, e.g., whether replacement therapy is likely to restore function and/or whether inhibitory antibodies are more likely to develop. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: According to the figure, state the type of gene mutation that is most frequently reported in severe haemophilia A.
parts.markscheme: - Intron 22 inversion. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify one mutation type exclusive to either haemophilia A or B as per the pie charts.
parts.markscheme:

_**OR**_

- Promoter mutation appears only in haemophilia B. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define Phenylketonuria (PKU)
steps.content: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited genetic disorder where the body is unable to break down an amino acid called phenylalanine. This is due to a deficiency in the enzyme **phenylalanine hydroxylase**.

highlights.text: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a genetic disorder characterized by a deficiency in the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase

steps.title: Explain metabolic role
steps.content: The enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase is essential for the metabolic process that converts the amino acid **phenylalanine** into another amino acid, **tyrosine**. In individuals with PKU, the lack of this enzyme causes phenylalanine to accumulate to toxic levels in the blood and brain.

highlights.text: which is necessary for the conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine

steps.title: Outline genetic basis
steps.content: The condition is caused by a mutation in the **$PAH$ gene**, which is located on chromosome 12. PKU is inherited in an **autosomal recessive** pattern, meaning an individual must inherit two copies of the mutated gene (one from each parent) to be affected.

highlights.text: It is caused by mutations in the $PAH$ gene located on chromosome 12, and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern

parts.content: Explain the consequences if left untreated and how it can be managed.
parts.markscheme:
**Consequences if left untreated**
- Intellectual disability <marks>1</marks>
- Developmental delays <marks>1</marks>
- Behavioral problems <marks>1</marks>
- Seizures due to accumulation of phenylalanine in the body <marks>1</marks>

**Management**
- Strict diet low in phenylalanine <marks>1</marks>
- Regular blood tests to monitor phenylalanine levels <marks>1</marks>
- Medication or supplements (in some cases) to help manage the condition <marks>1</marks>

<note>Max. 2 marks per section.</note>

steps.title: Recall the cause of PKU
steps.content:
To understand the consequences, we first recall that **Phenylketonuria (PKU)** is a genetic disorder where the body cannot produce the enzyme needed to break down the amino acid $phenylalanine$. 

This leads to a toxic buildup of $phenylalanine$ in the blood and brain. This concept is part of **Topic D3.2: Inheritance** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify untreated consequences
steps.content:
If PKU is left untreated from birth, the high levels of $phenylalanine$ cause severe damage to the central nervous system. The specific clinical symptoms and impairments include:

*   **Intellectual disability:** Permanent damage to cognitive function.
*   **Developmental delays:** Slowed physical and mental growth milestones.
*   **Behavioral problems:** Including hyperactivity or social anxieties.
*   **Seizures:** Resulting from the direct toxic effect on the brain.

Note: In the exam, you can earn a **maximum of 2 marks** for listing these consequences.

steps.title: Outline management protocols
steps.content:
Since PKU is a metabolic disorder, management focuses on controlling the intake of $phenylalanine$ and monitoring the body's levels. Standard protocols include:

*   **Strict low-protein diet:** Avoiding foods high in $phenylalanine$ (like meat, dairy, nuts, and certain artificial sweeteners).
*   **Regular blood tests:** To ensure $phenylalanine$ levels remain within a safe range.
*   **Specialized supplements:** Providing other necessary amino acids to ensure proper nutrition without the toxic buildup.

You can earn a **maximum of 2 marks** for these management points.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To get the full 3 marks, you should provide a balanced answer. For example:
1.  Mention **Intellectual disability** or **seizures** as a consequence.
2.  Mention **Developmental delays** or **behavioral issues** as a second consequence.
3.  Explain the **strict low-phenylalanine diet** as the primary management tool.

This ensures you meet the requirement of 3 marks while respecting the 2-mark cap per section.

steps.title: Understand the Metabolic Block
steps.content:
This question relates to **Topic D3.2 (Inheritance)**. Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by a mutation in the gene for the enzyme **phenylalanine hydroxylase**. In a healthy individual, this enzyme converts the amino acid phenylalanine into tyrosine. 

$$\text{Phenylalanine} \xrightarrow{\text{Phenylalanine hydroxylase}} \text{Tyrosine}$$

When this enzyme is absent or non-functional, the metabolic pathway is blocked, leading to a toxic accumulation of phenylalanine in the blood and tissues.

steps.title: Identify Neurological Consequences
steps.content:
From a biochemical perspective, the high concentration of phenylalanine is neurotoxic. It interferes with the transport of other essential amino acids to the brain and disrupts the synthesis of neurotransmitters and the formation of myelin. 

If left untreated, this results in significant neurological damage, which manifests as:
*   **Intellectual disability**
*   **Developmental delays**
*   **Behavioral problems**
*   **Seizures** (due to the direct effect of accumulated phenylalanine on brain activity)

highlights.text: Seizures due to accumulation of phenylalanine in the body

steps.title: Apply Substrate Restriction Management
steps.content:
Since the body cannot process phenylalanine, the rational biochemical management is **substrate restriction**. By limiting the intake of the "raw material" that the body cannot handle, we prevent the toxic buildup.

Key management strategies include:
*   A **strict diet low in phenylalanine**: This involves avoiding high-protein foods (like meat, dairy, and nuts) and using specialized medical formulas.
*   **Regular blood tests**: These are essential to monitor phenylalanine levels and ensure they remain within a safe, non-toxic range.

highlights.text: Regular blood tests to monitor phenylalanine levels

steps.title: Consider Supplemental Support
steps.content: In addition to diet, some patients may require specialized **medications or supplements**. Because the natural conversion to tyrosine is blocked, tyrosine becomes an "essential" amino acid for individuals with PKU and must be supplemented to ensure proper protein synthesis and neurotransmitter production.

highlights.text: Medication or supplements (in some cases) to help manage the condition

parts.content: Describe the process of fertilization in humans.
parts.markscheme:
- Sperm travels through the cervix and uterus to the oviduct (fallopian tube), where fertilization usually occurs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Sperm nucleus enters the egg cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Cortical reaction/changes to the egg membrane/zona pellucida prevent polyspermy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Zygote begins mitosis/cleavage and development <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the significance of meiosis in inheritance.

- Allows restoration of the diploid chromosome number after fertilization <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Crossing over in prophase I increases genetic variation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Independent assortment leads to different combinations of alleles in gametes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gametes are genetically varied/likely genetically different from each other <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contributes to diversity in offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides variation on which natural selection can act, contributing to adaptation and evolution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Errors in meiosis (e.g., nondisjunction) can lead to genetic disorders/aneuploidy <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: If a child is blood type O, both parents must carry the $i$ allele, so parents are $I^A i$ and $I^B i$. An AB child requires genotype $I^A I^B$, which occurs with probability $\frac{1}{4}=25\%$, so 0% is incorrect.

A child with blood type O has genotype $ii$, so each parent must contribute an $i$ allele: parents are $I^A i$ and $I^B i$. Crossing $I^A i \times I^B i$ gives offspring genotypes $I^A I^B$, $I^A i$, $I^B i$, $ii$ in a $1:1:1:1$ ratio. Therefore $P(AB)=\frac{1}{4}=25\%$.
With parents $I^A i$ and $I^B i$ (required because they have an O child), the probability of $I^A I^B$ (AB) is $\frac{1}{4}=25\%$, not 50%.
An AB child is not guaranteed. With parents $I^A i$ and $I^B i$, only $\frac{1}{4}$ of offspring are $I^A I^B$ (AB), so 100% is incorrect.

options.markscheme:
Haemophilia B is an $X$-linked recessive trait ($X^H$ = normal allele, $X^h$ = haemophilia allele).

1. Deducing Generation I genotypes: Individual II-1 is an affected male ($X^hY$). Since his father (I-1) is unaffected ($X^HY$), II-1 must have inherited the $X^h$ allele from his mother. Therefore, I-2 must be a carrier ($X^HX^h$).
2. Probability for Generation II: Individual II-2 is an unaffected daughter of a carrier mother ($X^HX^h$) and an unaffected father ($X^HY$). Daughters receive $X^H$ from the father and have a 50% chance of receiving $X^h$ from the mother, so $P(\text{II-2 is a carrier})=0.5$.
3. Probability for Generation III (offspring of unspecified sex): II-2 partners with an unaffected male (II-4, $X^HY$). If II-2 is a carrier, only sons can be affected because daughters receive $X^H$ from the father. Therefore $P(\text{affected} \mid \text{II-2 carrier})=P(\text{male})\times P(\text{inherits }X^h\text{ from mother})=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{4}$.
4. Total probability: $0.5\times 0.25=0.125=12.5\%$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.2.19 Autosomal gene linkage (HL)

options.content: Set 1 indicates linkage; Set 2 is consistent with independent assortment.
options.markscheme: In Set 1, the calculated $$\chi^2$$ value (14.21) exceeds the critical value (3.84), meaning the null hypothesis ($$H_0$$: the traits assort independently) is rejected at the 5% level, suggesting linkage. In Set 2, the calculated value (1.15) is less than the critical value, so the researcher fails to reject the null hypothesis, indicating the data is consistent with independent assortment.

options.content: Set 1 proves linkage; Set 2 proves genes are on different chromosomes.
options.markscheme: In statistical analysis, results provide evidence to support or reject a hypothesis but do not provide absolute 'proof'. Furthermore, genes that assort independently (Set 2) could still be located far apart on the same chromosome.

options.content: Both sets reject the null hypothesis because the same critical value was used.
options.markscheme: The null hypothesis in a chi-squared test for independence is that the genes are **unlinked** (assort independently), not that they are linked. Rejection of the null hypothesis occurs only when the calculated value exceeds the critical value, which did not happen for Set 2.

options.content: Set 2 has $p<0.05$, so it significantly departs from expected ratios.
options.markscheme: Because the calculated $$\chi^2$$ value for Set 2 (1.15) is less than the critical value (3.84), the $$p$$-value is greater than 0.05. This means there is a greater than 5% probability that the observed results occurred by chance under the null hypothesis of independent assortment.

What is the probability that their first two children will both have blood type A?

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires determining the man’s genotype from phenotype plus family information (father type O), i.e., identifying the combination of alleles inherited.

options.markscheme: This is the probability that a single child from this cross will have blood type A. The question asks for the probability of two consecutive children both having this phenotype ($0.5 \times 0.5$).

1. Determine genotypes: The man is blood type A but his father was type O ($ii$), so the man must be heterozygous ($I^Ai$). The woman is blood type AB ($I^AI^B$).
2. Perform the cross ($I^Ai \times I^AI^B$): The possible offspring genotypes are $I^AI^A$ (Type A), $I^AI^B$ (Type AB), $I^Ai$ (Type A), and $I^Bi$ (Type B).
3. Probability of Type A: Two out of four outcomes result in blood type A ($I^AI^A$ and $I^Ai$), which is $50\%$ or $0.5$ per child.
4. Probability for two children: Since the events are independent, the total probability is $0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$ or $25\%$.
This represents the probability of three consecutive children having blood type A ($0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.125$), or a calculation error involving a $25\%$ probability for a single child.

This would be the probability if the chance of a single child having blood type A was $25\%$ ($0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.0625$). This might occur if the student incorrectly assumed blood type A required the $I^AI^A$ genotype only.

A fly heterozygous for both traits was crossed with a fly that was homozygous recessive for both traits. The resulting offspring phenotypes were recorded in the table below:

| Offspring Phenotype | Number of Individuals |
| :--- | :--- |
| Grey body, long wings | 415 |
| Black body, short wings | 425 |
| Grey body, short wings | 78 |
| Black body, long wings | 82 |

Based on this data, what is the recombination frequency for these two gene loci?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.2 Inheritance > D3.2.20 Recombinants in crosses involving two linked or unlinked genes (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.2.20 Recombinants in crosses involving two linked or unlinked genes (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.2.19 Autosomal gene linkage (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes a dihybrid cross investigating the linkage between two autosomal genes (body color and wing length) in fruit flies, which is a classic example of autosomal gene linkage.

options.markscheme:
The recombination frequency is calculated by dividing the total number of recombinant offspring by the total number of offspring, then multiplying by 100.
- Recombinant phenotypes are those different from the parents: Grey body/short wings (78) and Black body/long wings (82).
- Total recombinants = $$78 + 82 = 160$$.
- Total offspring = $$415 + 425 + 78 + 82 = 1000$$.
- Recombination frequency = $$\frac{160}{1000} \times 100 = 16.0\%$$.

options.markscheme: This probability would be expected if one parent was genotype $I^A I^B$ and the other was $ii$, but in this cross ($I^A i \times I^B i$), the probability is different.
This would imply that all offspring must inherit both the $I^A$ and $I^B$ alleles, which is not possible since both parents are heterozygous and also carry the $i$ allele.

This would only be correct if one of the parents lacked the $I^A$ or $I^B$ allele entirely (e.g., if both were type O), but the parents' phenotypes and the child's phenotype prove the necessary alleles are present.

Because the first child has blood group O (genotype $ii$), both parents must be heterozygous carriers of the recessive allele ($i$). Therefore, the mother is $I^A i$ and the father is $I^B i$. A monohybrid cross between $I^A i$ and $I^B i$ yields a $25\%$ chance for each of the four possible phenotypes: A, B, AB, and O. Thus, the probability of an AB child ($I^A I^B$) is $0.25$.

parts.content: State the genetic cause of phenylketonuria.
parts.markscheme:
- State that PKU is caused by a mutation in an autosomal gene coding for the enzyme that converts phenylalanine to tyrosine <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept "mutation in the $PAH$ gene"</note>

parts.content: Explain why PKU is only expressed in individuals who are homozygous for the mutated allele.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that PKU is an autosomal recessive condition, meaning both alleles must carry the mutation to show symptoms <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that in heterozygotes, the normal allele produces enough functional phenylalanine hydroxylase for normal metabolism (haplosufficient) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link to phenotype: one functional allele prevents phenylalanine accumulation/toxic effects; symptoms arise when little/no functional enzyme is produced in homozygous recessive individuals <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable alternative wording: heterozygotes are carriers and typically asymptomatic <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept "to study region-specific effects of PKU on the brain"</note>

parts.content: Explain the biological basis for the variation observed in grain yield within a single variety of a cereal crop, such as wheat.
parts.markscheme:
- grain yield is an example of continuous variation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- continuous variation results in a complete range of phenotypes between two extremes/no distinct categories <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the trait is controlled by polygenic inheritance/the interaction of multiple genes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- alleles of these multiple genes have an additive effect on the final phenotype <marks>1 mark</marks>
- environmental factors (e.g., soil nitrogen levels, water availability, or light intensity) significantly influence the expression of the genes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the distribution of yield across the population typically follows a normal distribution curve <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 4 max</note>

parts.content: Explain how a population of bacteria can evolve resistance to an antibiotic and discuss the factors that could cause this change to happen rapidly.
parts.markscheme:
- evolution involves a change in the frequency of alleles in the bacterial population over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- genetic variation arises through spontaneous mutations (or horizontal gene transfer/plasmids) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the antibiotic acts as a strong selection pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- bacteria with resistance alleles have a selective advantage/higher fitness in the presence of the antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- surviving bacteria reproduce (binary fission) and pass the resistance alleles to their offspring/the next generation <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Factors for rapid evolution:**

- high frequency/misuse of antibiotics providing constant selection pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- short generation time of bacteria allows for rapid turnover of populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- horizontal gene transfer (via conjugation/transformation) allows for the rapid spread of resistance genes between individuals/species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- high mutation rates in some bacterial strains <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks; maximum 3 marks if no factors for rapid evolution are provided</note>

parts.content: Haemophilia A is a blood-clotting disorder that occurs more frequently in males than females. Describe the inheritance pattern of this condition.
parts.markscheme:
- it is a sex-linked trait/the gene is located on the non-homologous region of the X chromosome <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the allele for haemophilia is recessive to the normal clotting allele <marks>1 mark</marks>
- females can be carriers (heterozygous) without showing symptoms <marks>1 mark</marks>
- males are more likely to be affected because they only have one X chromosome/are hemizygous (so a single recessive allele causes the disorder) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- affected fathers cannot pass the trait to their sons (as they pass only the Y chromosome to sons) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 7 max</note>

parts.content: Using specific individuals in the pedigree, justify whether the allele responsible for this trait is inherited in a dominant or recessive manner.
parts.markscheme:
- **Deduce** that the trait is recessive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Justify** using individuals I-1 and I-2 (or III-1 and III-5): these parents are unaffected but produce an affected offspring (II-1 or IV-1), which is only possible if the trait is recessive and both parents are carriers <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable evidence (award as an alternative route, do not exceed total marks):
- **State** that the trait can skip generations (e.g. affected II-1, no affected individuals in generation III, affected IV-1) which supports recessive inheritance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that if the trait were dominant, at least one parent of an affected individual would be affected; this is not the case for II-1 (parents I-1 and I-2 are unaffected) or IV-1 (parents III-1 and III-5 are unaffected) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the evidence from the pedigree that rules out X-linked recessive inheritance for this trait.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that individual IV-1 (an affected female) has an unaffected father (III-5) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that in X-linked recessive inheritance, an affected female must inherit a recessive allele from both parents, so her father would have to be affected (as he has only one X chromosome) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

Alternative acceptable evidence (do not exceed total marks):
- **Identify** that individual II-1 is an affected male and his daughter III-1 is unaffected <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that in X-linked recessive inheritance, all daughters of an affected male receive his affected X chromosome and would therefore be at least carriers; if the mother is also a carrier, an affected daughter could occur, but IV-1 being affected with an unaffected father (III-5) rules out X-linked recessive <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify which four individuals in the lineage are definitively known to be heterozygous for this trait. Provide their generation and identification numbers.
parts.markscheme:
For a recessive trait, heterozygotes are unaffected carriers.

- **Identify** I-1 as heterozygous (unaffected but has affected child II-1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** I-2 as heterozygous (unaffected but has affected child II-1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** III-1 as heterozygous (unaffected child of affected father II-1, so must have inherited the recessive allele) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** III-4 as heterozygous (unaffected child of affected father II-1, so must have inherited the recessive allele) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** III-5 as heterozygous (unaffected but has affected child IV-1) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award:
- Any four correct identifications from the above list <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Two or three correct identifications from the above list <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

Accept any equivalent set of four from: I-1, I-2, III-1, III-4, III-5 <marks>2 marks</marks>
